title: CSS Gradient Generator (linear, radial, conic)
shortDescription: Build CSS gradients visually: linear, radial, and conic. Multiple color stops, angle control, presets. Copy ready-to-paste CSS.
description: Pick a gradient type, set angle, add or remove color stops with positions. Live preview. Copy the CSS background declaration with one click.
metaDescription: Free CSS gradient generator with live preview. Linear, radial, and conic gradients. Multi-stop, angle control, ready-to-copy CSS code.

sections.heading: Three gradient types in CSS
paragraphs: Linear gradient: colors transition along a straight line at a given angle. 0° points up, 90° points right (default reading direction), 180° points down. Most common for backgrounds, buttons, hero sections.
Radial gradient: colors transition outward from a center point. Shape can be a circle (our default) or ellipse. Best for spotlight effects, sun-like elements, or attention-drawing centerpieces.
Conic gradient: colors rotate around a center point, like a color wheel. Less common but powerful for pie charts, clock faces, or decorative spinners. Supported in modern browsers since 2020.

sections.heading: Color stops and the percentage system
paragraphs: Each color stop has a color and a position from 0% to 100%. 0% is the start of the gradient, 100% is the end. Two stops give a smooth two-color gradient. Adding a third stop in the middle creates a tri-color blend or a sharp band depending on positioning.

For sharp transitions (no blending), put two stops at the same position with different colors. For example, '#000 50%, #fff 50%' creates a sharp black-to-white split at the midpoint. This is how 'striped' backgrounds are made.

sections.heading: Performance and accessibility tips
paragraphs: Gradients render natively without HTTP requests, unlike background images. They scale infinitely without quality loss. They're animation-friendly: transition the position via background-position or animate gradient stops with custom properties.

Accessibility: don't put text directly over a complex gradient if contrast varies across the gradient. Either use a flat color overlay, ensure the gradient's average lightness contrasts with the text, or test with our contrast checker. WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text.

howTo.name: Pick a preset
howTo.text: Or build from scratch by choosing type, angle, and stops.

howTo.name: Set the angle
howTo.text: For linear and conic: 0-360°. Radial uses center as origin.

howTo.name: Add color stops
howTo.text: Two minimum, no practical maximum. Each has a color and a 0-100% position.

howTo.name: Copy the CSS
howTo.text: Click 'Copy CSS' — paste directly into your stylesheet's background property.

faq.q: Are gradients supported in old browsers?
faq.a: Linear and radial gradients work in all modern browsers including Edge, Firefox, Chrome, Safari (since 2014). Conic gradients require Chrome 69+, Firefox 83+, Safari 12.1+ — for older browsers, fall back to linear.

faq.q: Can I use these in React Native or mobile apps?
faq.a: Web CSS gradients don't translate to React Native directly — use libraries like `react-native-linear-gradient`. The color stops translate, but the syntax differs. iOS and Android UIs use platform-specific APIs.
Yes. Replace any hex code (e.g. #ff0000) with the CSS color name (red, dodgerblue, etc.) in the text input next to the color picker.

faq.q: Why does my conic gradient look weird?
faq.a: Conic gradients rotate clockwise from the angle you set. They look best with multiple stops covering the full 360° rotation. With only 2 stops they often look incomplete.

faq.q: How do I create a 'glow' effect?
faq.a: Use a radial gradient with a fully transparent end stop: e.g., 'radial-gradient(circle, rgba(255,200,0,0.6) 0%, transparent 70%)'.
CSS doesn't animate gradient stops directly, but you can animate background-position for a moving effect, or use CSS custom properties (variables) and update them with JavaScript.

faq.q: Why is the CSS so long?
faq.a: Each stop adds a color and position. Long gradients with many stops produce verbose CSS. For simpler results, stick to 2-3 stops.

faq.q: Does the data leave my browser?
faq.a: No. Everything is computed locally; nothing is sent to a server.
